title: FVIC (Frequency of Visibly Infected Cells) Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"64qkm","text":"Purpose: This protocol describes how to pellet bacterial cells onto grids and stain them so that viruses can be visualized within the cells using transmission electron microscopy. Data obtained from these grids can be used to calculate the frequency of visibly infected cells (FVIC), the frequency of infected cells (FIC), the fraction of mortality due to viral lysis (FMVL), and burst size.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"38fdq","text":"*Caution: uranyl acetate is toxic so read the MSDS and use a lab coat, eye protection, respiratory mask, and gloves when handling it in powdered form.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: On the same day of grid prep, use glow discharge to render grids hydrophilic.
Cut a circle out of a paper filter divider such that it has a diameter slightly smaller than the interior diameter of the ultracentrifuge tubes.
Cut small rectangles (~3 mm x 1 mm) of double-sided tape using a razor blade.

Attach the discharged grids (shiny-side-up) to the circle of paper using the double-sided tape such that only the edge of the grids contacts the tape (Figure 1) a Figure 1:Grids attached to the paper filter divider with double-sided tape.
Insert the paper with the attached grids into the ultracentrifuge tube grids-side-up (Figure 2) a Figure 2. Grids inserted into the bottom of the ultracentrifuge tube.

Add your sample to the ultracentrifuge tube ensuring that there are no air bubbles under the circle of paper. Note: ca. 20 ml of sample is required for samples with bacterial concentrations of 10 7 ml -1 . Adjust the sample volume according to the bacterial concentration in your samples.

Centrifuge samples using the following conditions (you can use a different rotor as long as it can reach the appropriate g-force): Rotor: Beckman SW 40 Ti Temperature: 4ºC Time: 1 hr Speed: 20000 rpm (average force = 55000 xg) Balance the rotor exactly with another sample or dummy sample.
After centrifugation, you can remove the sample and add more sample for additional centrifugation runs to achieve the required volume .

Make 2 ml of 0.5% uranyl acetate with Q-water in a 4 ml culture tube using a stir bar to mix the sample on a stir plate for ~30 minutes to 1 hour. 01:00:00 Do not turn on the heat! 0.01 g uranyl acetate + 2 ml Q-water = 0.5% uranyl acetate Caution: uranyl acetate is toxic so read the MSDS and use a lab coat, eye protection, respiratory mask, and gloves when handling it in powdered form
Filter the uranyl acetate solution through a 0.02 µm syringe filter into a 2 ml screw-cap tube. This removes any particles of uranyl acetate that have not fully dissolved.
Fill 3x 2 ml screw-cap tubes with Q-water.
Using tweezers, remove the piece of paper with the attached grids from the ultracentrifuge tube and place in a petri dish.
Using tweezers, remove a grid from the piece of paper (make sure the tape is not still attached to the grid).
Immerse the grid into the 0.5% uranyl acetate solution for 30 seconds (Figure 3) a Figure 3. Immersing the grid in the uranyl acetate solution. 00:00:30
Immerse the grid into the 1st tube of Q-water for 10 seconds. 00:00:10
Immerse the grid into the 2nd tube of Q-water for 10 seconds 00:00:10
Immerse the grid into the 3rd tube of Q-water for 10 seconds. 00:00:10
Wick away liquid from the grid using a wedge of filter paper placed at the edge of the grid (Figure 4) a

Place the grid in a grid box and allow to dry overnight, then store the grid in a desiccator until analysis. Do not put wet grids in a desiccator immediately as this can create “cracking” of the stain.
Using a transmission electron microscope with 100 kV accelerating voltage, examine a grid at ~40000X.

Look at 800 cells per grid. Count how many cells have >5 viruses assembled within the cell (Figure 4) a Figure 4. Examples of cells that would be counted as visibly infected and not visibly infected. Greater than 5” rule prevents you from mistaking cellular structures or extracellular viruses from intracellular viruses.
Take a digital micrograph of all visibly infected cells.
Calculate the frequency of visibly infected cells (FVIC): a
Calculate the frequency of infected cells (FIC): a
Calculate the fraction of mortality from viral lysis (FMVL): a

Determine burst size: Using the micrographs of visibly infected cells, count how many viruses are present inside of cells that are full of viruses. *Caveats: - some cells can burst before they are full of viruses (thus, this is an overestimate of burst size) - it may be impossible to count the total number of viruses within larger cells because they are overlapping one another (thus, this is an underestimate of burst size)
Other information that can be obtained using ImageJ or other image analysis software: - dimensions of infected cells - dimensions of intracellular viruses
